patient_profile.hpi: Mr. O'Connell presents to the clinic reporting intermittent palpitations for the last 2 weeks. He states, 'It feels like a bird is trapped in my chest.' He denies chest pain. He has a history of 'mild AFib' dating back 5 years but stopped taking his blood thinner (Apixaban) 6 months ago due to cost. He reports feeling increasingly fatigued and short of breath when climbing stairs.
patient_profile.pmh: Paroxysmal Atrial Fibrillation, Hypertension, Hyperlipidemia, Obstructive Sleep Apnea.
patient_profile.sh: Retired police officer. Social drinker (2-3 beers/day). Smoker (1 pack/day). Low sodium diet (non-compliant).

ros.cardio: Irregularly irregular rhythm. HR 110-120bpm. No murmurs.
ros.resp: Mild dyspnea on exertion. Lungs clear.
ros.neuro: Alert & Oriented x4. No facial droop or slurred speech.
ros.gi: Abdomen soft, non-tender. Bowel sounds active.
ros.gen: Temp 98.6°F, HR 72, RR 16, BP 120/80, O2 Sat 98% on RA

exhibits.title: ECG Strip (10:00)
exhibits.content:
Rhythm: Atrial Fibrillation with Rapid Ventricular Response (RVR).
Rate: 118 bpm.
Intervals: Absent P waves. Fibrillatory waves present. QRS narrow (0.08s).

exhibits.title: Echocardiogram Report
exhibits.content: Left Atrial Enlargement. LVEF 50%. No left atrial appendage thrombus visualized.

steps.prompt: The nurse admits Mr. O'Connell. Which assessment findings are consistent with Atrial Fibrillation with RVR? Select all that apply.
steps.rationale: AFib is characterized by chaotic atrial activity (no P waves) and an irregular ventricular response. RVR implies a rate > 100. A pulse deficit is common as not all beats generate enough pressure to be palpable peripherally. QRS is typically narrow unless there is aberrant conduction.

steps.prompt: The provider orders medications. For each drug, indicate the primary purpose in this scenario.
steps.rationale: Diltiazem and Metoprolol are rate control agents (slowing AV conduction). Heparin prevents thrombus formation (stroke prevention). Amiodarone is an antiarrhythmic used to restore/maintain sinus rhythm (rhythm control).

steps.prompt: The patient suddenly develops slurred speech and right-sided weakness. Prioritize the immediate response.
steps.rationale: The greatest risk in AFib is embolic stroke (clot leaving the LA appendage). Slurred speech and weakness are classic signs. Immediate action is to call a Stroke Code and get a CT to rule out hemorrhage. Glucose is checked to rule out hypoglycemia mimics. tPA is NOT given until hemorrhage is ruled out.

patient_profile.hpi: Ms. Jenkins came to the ER with a pounding headache (8/10) and epistaxis that wouldn't stop for 20 mins. She ran out of her 'blood pressure pills' 4 days ago. She denies chest pain, shortness of breath, or blurred vision.

ros.resp: Lungs clear to auscultation bilaterally. No distress.
ros.gi: Abdomen soft, non-tender. Bowel sounds active.
ros.gen: Temp 98.6°F, HR 72, RR 16, BP 120/80, O2 Sat 98% on RA

exhibits.title: Urinalysis
exhibits.content: Protein: Negative. Blood: Negative. Glucose: Negative. (No renal damage)

steps.prompt: The nurse assesses Ms. Jenkins. Determine which findings indicate 'Target Organ Damage' vs 'No Target Organ Damage'.
steps.rationale: Urgency is defined by BP > 180/120 WITHOUT organ damage. Headache and epistaxis are symptoms but not organ 'damage' like MI (chest pain), renal failure (high Cr), or stroke.

steps.prompt: Based on the assessment (High BP, No Organ Damage), prioritize the care plan.
steps.rationale: The diagnosis is Urgency. Treatment involves ORAL agents (Clonidine/Captopril) to lower BP gradually over 24-48h. IV agents are for Emergency. Reducing stimuli assists in lowering BP. Neuro checks ensure no progression to stroke.

steps.prompt: Ms. Jenkins is discharged. Which statements require further teaching? Select all that apply.
options: I can stop the medication when my headache goes away.
I should avoid salty foods like canned soups.
If I miss a dose, I will double up the next day.
I will buy a home blood pressure cuff.
I will schedule a follow-up with my PCP in 1 week.
steps.rationale: Hypertension is a silent, lifelong condition. Medication adherence is crucial even when asymptomatic. Doubling doses can cause dangerous hypotension.

patient_profile.chief_complaint: Swollen legs and can't breathe when lying down.
patient_profile.hpi: Mr. White reports increasing edema over the last week. He has been sleeping in a recliner (3-pillow orthopnea). He admits to eating ham and pizza for the Super Bowl 2 days ago.
patient_profile.pmh: Heart Failure (HFrEF - EF 35%), MI x2, Hyperlipidemia.

ros.resp: Crackles in bilateral bases. Doze 34. SpO2 90% RA.
ros.gen: Temp 98.6°F, HR 72, RR 16, BP 120/80, O2 Sat 98% on RA

patient_profile.sh: Software engineer. Works from home. High stress. Enjoys hiking.

exhibits.title: Chest X-Ray
exhibits.content: Cardiomegaly. Pulmonary vascular congestion. Bilateral pleural effusions.

steps.prompt: Differentiate the patient's symptoms between Right-Sided and Left-Sided Heart Failure.
steps.rationale: Left HF backs up into the Lungs (Crackles, Orthopnea). Right HF backs up into the Rest of the body (JVD, Ascites, Edema). This patient has biventricular failure.

steps.prompt: The patient becomes increasingly dyspneic, SpO2 drops to 85%, and he starts coughing pink frothy sputum. Take immediate action.
steps.rationale: Pink frothy sputum is pathognomonic for Flash Pulmonary Edema. This is an AIRWAY emergency. Diuretics (Lasix) remove fluid. Positive pressure ventilation (BiPAP) pushes fluid out of alveoli. Laying flat kills the patient.

steps.prompt: The patient stabilizes. Which discharge instructions are appropriate? Select all that apply.
options: Weigh yourself daily at the same time
Restrict fluid intake to 1.5 - 2L per day
Limit Sodium intake to < 2g per day
Take Ibuprofen for aches and pains
Call provider if you gain 3 lbs in a day or 5 lbs in a week
steps.rationale: Daily weights are the best indicator of fluid status. NSAIDs (Ibuprofen) cause sodium retention and are contraindicated in HF.

patient_profile.hpi: Ms. Stewart reports a 3-month history of chest tightness when walking uphill, which she attributed to 'old age'. Today, she felt lightheaded while gardening in the sun and woke up on the grass. Her daughter witnessed the syncope and called 911. She denies palpitations or incontinence.
patient_profile.sh: Retired librarian. Lives with daughter. Active gardener.

ros.cardio: Harsh systolic crescendo-decrescendo murmur at 2nd Intercostal space, Right Sternal Border. Radiates to carotids. Delayed carotid upstroke.
ros.resp: Mild dyspnea on exertion. Lungs clear.
ros.gi: Abdomen soft, non-tender. Bowel sounds active.
ros.gen: Temp 98.6°F, HR 72, RR 16, BP 120/80, O2 Sat 98% on RA

exhibits.title: Echocardiogram
exhibits.content:
Finding: Severe Calcific Aortic Stenosis.
Valve Area: 0.8 cm² (Critical < 1.0).
Mean Gradient: 45 mmHg (High).
LVEF: 55% with concentric LV hypertrophy.

exhibits.title: ECG
exhibits.content: Normal Sinus Rhythm at 72 bpm. Left Ventricular Hypertrophy (Voltage criteria met).

steps.prompt: The nurse auscultates the patient's heart sounds. Differentiate the murmurs based on location and timing.
rows: Systolic Murmur at Apex (radiation to axilla)
Diastolic Murmur at Left Sternal Border
steps.rationale: Aortic Stenosis is a systolic ejection murmur heard best at the aortic area (R 2nd ICS). Mitral Regurgitation is a pansystolic murmur at the apex. Aortic Regurgitation is a diastolic decrescendo murmur.

steps.prompt: Ms. Stewart is admitted for TAVR workup. Which nursing interventions are appropriate for her safety? Select all that apply.
options: Encourage vigorous walking to build stamina
Instruct to change positions slowly (dangle legs)
Administer Nitroglycerin SL for chest pain
steps.rationale: Patients with fixed outlet obstruction (AS) are preload dependent. Nitroglycerin causes vasodilation, dropping preload and potentially causing fatal hypotension/syncope. Vigorous exercise increases demand that the heart cannot meet. Valsalva reduces preload.

steps.prompt: The patient reports chest pressure (Angina). The nurse prepares to act.
steps.rationale: In AS, the hypertrophied muscle needs more oxygen, but the valve limits flow. Nitro is risky! Oxygen increases supply. Rest (Semi-Fowlers) decreases demand. BP must be preserved to push blood through the stenotic valve.

patient_profile.hpi: Mr. Durant recovered from a 'bad flu' 2 weeks ago. Now presents with retrosternal chest pain that is 9/10, sharp, and pleuritic (worse with deep breath). He states, 'It feels better when I sit up and lean forward.'

ros.cardio: High-pitched scratching sound heard at Left Lower Sternal Border (Friction Rub).
ros.gi: Abdomen soft, non-tender. Bowel sounds active.

patient_profile.sh: Construction worker. Lives alone. Smokes 1 pack/day. Social drinker.

exhibits.title: ECG (12-Lead)
exhibits.content: Diffuse ST-segment elevation in all precordial leads (concave up). PR-segment depression in Lead II.

steps.prompt: Differentiate Pericarditis from Myocardial Infarction based on clinical presentation.
steps.rationale: Pericarditis pain is positional and pleuritic (inflammatory). MI pain is ischemic and often radiates. Diffuse vs Localized ST elevation is the key EKG discriminator.

steps.prompt: Which medications does the nurse anticipate administering? Select all that apply.
steps.rationale: High dose NSAIDs (Ibuprofen/Aspirin) are first line. Colchicine is added to reduce recurrence. Steroids (Prednisone) are second-line as they increase recurrence risk. Anticoagulants (Heparin) are contraindicated due to risk of hemopericardium (bleeding into the sac).

steps.prompt: The patient suddenly develops jugular venous distention, muffled heart sounds, and hypotension. Prioritize the complication.
steps.rationale: Beck's Triad (JVD, Muffled Sounds, Hypotension) confirms Tamponade. The fluid compresses the heart. Pericardiocentesis (draining the fluid) is the cure. IV fluids maximize preload to maintain output temporarily. Pulsus Paradoxus (drop in SBP > 10 during inspiration) is the classic sign.

patient_profile.hpi: Mr. De Niro presents with burning epigastric pain and aching in his jaw that started after a heavy meal. He took Tums with no relief. He denies 'crushing' chest pain but feels nauseous and sweaty.
patient_profile.pmh: Type 2 Diabetes, Hypertension, Smoker (30 pack years).

ros.resp: Lungs clear to auscultation bilaterally. No distress.

patient_profile.sh: Office manager. Married with 2 children. Nonsmoker. Denies alcohol use.

exhibits.title: ECG (12-Lead)
exhibits.content: Sinus Rhythm. ST-segment depression in leads V4-V6. T-wave inversion in II, III, aVF. (No ST Elevation).

steps.prompt: Differentiate NSTEMI from STEMI and Unstable Angina based on the findings.
steps.rationale: NSTEMI and STEMI both have positive troponins (cell death). STEMI has ST Elevation (full thickness blockage). NSTEMI has Depression/Inversion (partial blockage). Unstable Angina has pain but NEGATIVE troponins.

steps.prompt: The patient has an allergy to Aspirin (GI Upset). Which actions are appropriate? Select all that apply.
options: Pre-medicate with Famotidine (Pepcid) and give Aspirin
steps.rationale: GI Upset is an intolerance, not anaphylaxis, but typically if severe, we switch to Plavix (Clopidogrel). Heparin prevents clot growth. Nitro dilates coronaries. We generally don't force Aspirin if the patient reports bad reactions unless the provider overrides.

steps.prompt: The patient's pain increases to 10/10 despite 3 nitroglycerins. BP drops to 88/50. Prioritize.
steps.rationale: Inferior/Posterior mis (leads II, III, aVF) often involve the Right Ventricle. RV infarcts are PRELOAD DEPENDENT. Nitro drops preload and causes hypotension. The treatment is FLUIDS to push blood through the right side.

patient_profile.hpi: Ms. Rigby felt deeply dizzy while picking up rice in the church. She had to sit down to avoid fainting. She reports generalized fatigue for a week.

ros.resp: Lungs clear to auscultation bilaterally. No distress.
ros.gi: Abdomen soft, non-tender. Bowel sounds active.

patient_profile.sh: Retired veteran. Lives in assisted living. Former smoker (quit 20 years ago).

exhibits.title: ECG Strip
exhibits.content:
Rate: Atrial 80 bpm, Ventricular 34 bpm.
Rhythm: P waves regular. QRS complexes regular. NO association between P and QRS. (P waves march through QRS).

steps.prompt: Identify the type of AV Block based on the description.
rows: PR interval constant, dropping QRS occasionally
PR interval lengthens until QRS drops
Complete dissociation of P and QRS
steps.rationale: 3rd degree block is a complete divorce between Atria and Ventricles. The ventricles rely on a slow escape rhythm (20-40 bpm). Wenckebach gives a warning (lengthening PR). Type II drops without warning.

steps.prompt: The nurse acts on the bradycardia. Which interventions are indicated? Select all that apply.
steps.rationale: Pacing is the definitive treatment for 3rd degree block. Atropine *may* be tried but often fails in high blocks. Dopamine increases heart rate/BP. Adenosine/Metoprolol slow the heart further and are CONTRAINDICATED.

steps.prompt: Ms. Rigby becomes unresponsive. No palpable pulse. Monitor shows the same rhythm (PEA). Prioritize.
steps.rationale: PEA is a rhythm on the monitor but NO pulse. You CANNOT shock PEA. You must do CPR and give Epi. H's and T's (Hypovolemia, Hypoxia, etc.) must be ruled out.

patient_profile.hpi: Mr. Jones reports he gets winded walking to the mailbox. He used to sing powerfully but now lacks the breath. He has a history of Rheumatic Fever as a child.

ros.cardio: Holosystolic murmur heard best at Apex, radiating to Axilla.
ros.gi: Abdomen soft, non-tender. Bowel sounds active.

patient_profile.sh: Librarian. Lives with 3 cats. Nonsmoker. Occasional glass of wine.

exhibits.title: Echocardiogram
exhibits.content: Severe Mitral Regurgitation. Regurgitant fraction 55%. Dilated Left Atrium. LVEF 45%.

steps.prompt: Correlate the pathophysiology of Mitral Regurgitation with the clinical findings.
rows: Blood flows backward from LV to LA
steps.rationale: The leak (murmur) causes backflow into the LA, which backs up into lungs (dyspnea). The stretched LA disrupts electrical pathways (AFib). The forward flow is reduced (Fatigue).

steps.prompt: Mr. Jones is scheduled for Mitral Valve Repair. Which pre-op orders are appropriate? Select all that apply.
steps.rationale: Diuretics manage the heart failure symptoms. Anticoagulation prevents stroke from AFib. Dental clearance is MANDATORY before valve surgery to prevent Endocarditis from oral bacteria. Antibiotics are given *intra-op*, not days before.

steps.prompt: Post-op, Mr. Jones has a mechanical valve. He asks about his lifestyle. Prioritize the education.
steps.rationale: Mechanical valves last forever but clot easily. They require LIFELONG Warfarin with a higher INR target (2.5-3.5). The valve makes a distinct 'clicking' sound. Antibiotic prophylaxis is needed for invasive procedures.

patient_profile.hpi: Mr. Eastwood complains of a dull ache in his lower back and abdomen that is not affected by movement. He says he feels 'a heartbeat in his stomach'.

ros.gi: Pulsatile mass palpable in periumbilical area.
ros.cardio: Bruit heard over abdominal aorta. BP 170/90.
ros.resp: Lungs clear to auscultation bilaterally. No distress.
ros.gen: Temp 98.6°F, HR 72, RR 16, BP 120/80, O2 Sat 98% on RA

patient_profile.sh: Nursing home resident. Widowed. Assistance required for ADLs.

exhibits.title: Ultrasound Abdomen
exhibits.content: Fusiform aneurysm of the abdominal aorta measuring 5.8 cm in diameter.

steps.prompt: The nurse assesses the patient. Identify signs of Rupture vs Stable Aneurysm.
steps.rationale: A stable AAA presents with a mass and bruit. Rupture causes massive internal bleeding -> Shock (Low BP, High HR), severe tearing pain, and bruising on the flanks (Grey Turner's).

steps.prompt: The AAA is 5.8 cm (surgical threshold). Mr. Eastwood is pre-op. What are the priority actions? Select all that apply.
options: Administer IV fluids bolus to increase pressure
Palpate the mass deeply to assess size
steps.rationale: Priority #1: Prevent rupture by lowering BP (Anti-impulse therapy). DEEP PALPATION IS CONTRAINDICATED (can rupture it). Pulses establish a baseline. Renal function is key as the repair involves clamping above renal arteries.

steps.prompt: Post-EVAR (Endovascular Repair), the patient's urine output drops to 10ml/hr and BP rises to 190/100. Prioritize.
steps.rationale: A sudden drop in urine output post-AAA repair suggests the stent graft may have migrated and covered the renal arteries. This is a SURGICAL EMERGENCY. High BP puts stress on the graft site and must be controlled (Labetalol). Creatinine confirms renal hit.

patient_profile.hpi: Patient with End Stage Renal Disease (ESRD) missed dialysis yesterday. He reports feeling 'heavy' in his legs and having a fluttering chest.

ros.resp: Lungs clear to auscultation bilaterally. No distress.
ros.gen: Temp 98.6°F, HR 72, RR 16, BP 120/80, O2 Sat 98% on RA

patient_profile.sh: Chef. High sodium diet. Smokes cigars occasionally. Social drinker.

exhibits.title: ECG
exhibits.content: Tall, peaked T-waves. Widened QRS complex. Provisid Prolonged PR interval.

steps.prompt: Prioritize the medications for Hyperkalemia based on their mechanism of action.
steps.rationale: Remember 'C Big K Drop'. Calcium protects the heart (FIRST priority if EKG changes). Insulin/Albuterol hide K+ in the cell. Kayexalate/Lasix/Dialysis actually remove it.

steps.prompt: The nurse prepares the Insulin/Dextrose cocktail. Which safety checks are required? Select all that apply.
options: Check Blood Glucose prior to administration
Push Dextrose slowly to avoid vein irritation
Monitor BG every hour after administration
Give Insulin first, then wait 30 mins for Dextrose
steps.rationale: Only Regular insulin can be given IV. Dextrose is given WITH insulin to prevent hypoglycemia. Vein irritation is common with D50. Hypoglycemia is the main risk, so hourly checks are mandatory.

steps.prompt: Despite meds, the QRS widens to 0.20s and heart rate drops to 30. Patient loses consciousness. Prioritize.
steps.rationale: Widening QRS signals impending sine-wave and asystole. Meds failed. CRP keeps him alive until Dialysis (the only thing that will pull the K+ fast enough) can be started. This is a Code Blue situation.

patient_profile.hpi: Mr. Dent had a massive Anterior MI 2 days ago. He was stable initially but the nurse now finds him confused, with cold/clammy skin and decreased urine output.

ros.gi: Abdomen soft, non-tender. Bowel sounds active.

patient_profile.sh: Chef. High sodium diet. Smokes cigars occasionally. Social drinker.

steps.prompt: Distinguish Cardiogenic Shock from Hypovolemic Shock based on the findings.
steps.rationale: Both shocks cause low BP/high HR/cold skin. The differentiator is FLUID STATUS. Cardiogenic is WET (Crackles, High Wedge). Hypovolemic is DRY (Flat veins, Low Wedge).

steps.prompt: The provider orders Dobutamine. Which actions are consistent with this therapy? Select all that apply.
options: Expect a decrease in cardiac contractility
Administer via central line if possible
Give with Beta Bockers to prevent tachycardia
steps.rationale: Dobutamine is an inotrope (squeuzer). It INCREASES contractility but can irritate the heart (arrhythmias). It is a vesicant (central line preferred). Beta blockers would oppose its effect.

steps.prompt: Despite Dobutamine, BP drops to 70/40. The patient is obtunded. Prioritize the next level of support.
steps.rationale: Medical management failed. The heart needs mechanical help. IABP reduces afterload and increases coronary perfusion. Fluids kill this patient (lungs are full). Epi (Levophed preferred) may be needed for severe hypotension. IABP carries risk of limb ischemia.

patient_profile.hpi: Mr. Ranger reports 'burning' in his calves when he walks more than 1 block. The pain stops exactly 2 minutes after he sits down (Intermittent Claudication).

ros.skin: Legs are shiny, hairless, and cool to touch. Toenails are thickened.
ros.resp: Lungs clear to auscultation bilaterally. No distress.
ros.gi: Abdomen soft, non-tender. Bowel sounds active.
ros.gen: Temp 98.6°F, HR 72, RR 16, BP 120/80, O2 Sat 98% on RA

patient_profile.sh: Librarian. Lives with 3 cats. Nonsmoker. Occasional glass of wine.

steps.prompt: Detailed assessment of the legs. Classify findings as Arterial (PAD) or Venous (PVD).
steps.rationale: Arterial = Problem getting blood DOWN (Gravity helps, Elevation hurts). Venous = Problem getting blood UP (Elevation helps, Gravity hurts).

steps.prompt: Which instructions are appropriate for Mr. Ranger? Select all that apply.
options: Apply heating pads to feet for warmth
Walk until pain starts, rest, then walk again
steps.rationale: Heating pads are dangerous due to decreased sensation (burn risk). Compression stockings constrict arteries further (contraindicated in PAD, good for Venous). Walking builds collateral circulation. Crossing legs obstructs flow.

steps.prompt: Mr. Ranger calls reporting his right foot is suddenly cold, numb, and 'white'. He cannot move his toes. Prioritize.
steps.rationale: This is the '6 P's' emergency. A clot has completely blocked the artery. The limb will die in hours without blood. Immediate surgical (embolectomy) or medical (TPA) intervention is needed. Elevation makes ischemia WORSE.

patient_profile.hpi: Mr. Snow is a heavy smoker. He reports his fingers turn white, then blue, then red when he goes outside. Recently, he developed small ulcers on his fingertips that won't heal.

ros.skin: Gangrenous updates on right index and middle fingertips.
ros.cardio: Radial pulses palpable. Ulnar pulses weak.
ros.resp: Lungs clear to auscultation bilaterally. No distress.
ros.gi: Abdomen soft, non-tender. Bowel sounds active.
ros.gen: Temp 98.6°F, HR 72, RR 16, BP 120/80, O2 Sat 98% on RA

patient_profile.sh: Nursing home resident. Widowed. Assistance required for ADLs.

exhibits.title: Angiogram
exhibits.content: Corkscrew collateral vessels in the hands (Pathognomonic for Buerger's Disease).

steps.prompt: Compare Raynaud's Phenomenon vs Buerger's Disease (Thromboangiitis Obliterans).
rows: Review of Systems: Vasospasm triggered by cold/stress
Review of Systems: Inflammation/Clotting of small vessels
steps.rationale: Raynaud's is a functional spasm (rarely causes tissue loss unless severe). Buerger's is an inflammatory clotting disease EXCLUSIVELY linked to smoking that destroys distal vessels.

steps.prompt: The patient asks how to save his fingers. Which instructions are valid? Select all that apply.
options: You MUST stop smoking completely (even patches/gum are bad)
Wear gloves when reaching into the freezer
Take Calcium Channel Blockers (Nifedipine) as prescribed
steps.rationale: For Buerger's, total nicotine cessation is the ONLY cure. Even NRT (patches) can perpetuate it. Caffeine is a vasoconstrictor (avoid!). CCBs prevent vasospasm.

steps.prompt: Mr. Snow presents with throbbing pain in his hand. The finger is black and smells foul. Prioritize.
steps.rationale: Wet gangrene (foul smell, black) implies infection on top of dead tissue. This is a sepsis risk. The dead tissue must be removed (amputation). Antibiotics cover the infection.

patient_profile.chief_complaint: Sudden onset 'tearing' chest pain radiating to back.
patient_profile.hpi: Patient was lifting weights when he felt a 'rip' in his chest that shot between his shoulder blades. Pain is 10/10.

ros.cardio: BP Right Arm: 190/100. BP Left Arm: 140/80. (Pulse Deficit).
ros.resp: Lungs clear to auscultation bilaterally. No distress.
ros.gi: Abdomen soft, non-tender. Bowel sounds active.

patient_profile.sh: Truck driver. Divorced. Smokes 2 packs/day. Sedentary lifestyle.

exhibits.title: Chest CT Angio
exhibits.content: Type A Dissection (Ascending Aorta) extending to the arch. False lumen visualized.

steps.prompt: Compare Myocardial Infarction vs Aortic Dissection presentation.
steps.rationale: The hallmark of dissection is 'tearing' pain to the back and unequal BPs (because the tear blocks flow to one subclavian artery).

steps.prompt: The diagnosis is Type A Dissection (Surgical Emergency). While waiting for the OR, what are the nursing priorities? Select all that apply.
steps.rationale: We must reduce shear stress (dP/dt). Beta blockers (Esmolol) are given FIRST to lower heart rate. Then Vasodilators (Nitroprusside) to lower BP. If you give Nitro first, reflex tachycardia will tear the aorta further. Heparin causes fatal bleeding.

steps.prompt: Suddenly, BP drops to 60/40, JVD appears, and heart sounds become muffled. Prioritize.
steps.rationale: The dissection has ruptured retrograde into the pericardium (Tamponade). This is usually fatal without instant drainage/repair. Beck's triad (JVD, Hypotension, Muffled sounds) is the key.

patient_profile.hpi: Patient reports 2 weeks of high fevers, night sweats, and joint pain. He admits to IV drug use (Heroin).

ros.skin: Splinter hemorrhages under fingernails. Painful red nodes on fingers (Osler's Nodes). Painless spots on palms (Janeway Lesions).
ros.resp: Lungs clear to auscultation bilaterally. No distress.
ros.gi: Abdomen soft, non-tender. Bowel sounds active.
ros.gen: Temp 98.6°F, HR 72, RR 16, BP 120/80, O2 Sat 98% on RA

patient_profile.sh: Retired veteran. Lives in assisted living. Former smoker (quit 20 years ago).

exhibits.title: Blood Cultures
exhibits.content: Positive for Staphylococcus Aureus (MRSA) in 2/2 bottles.

exhibits.title: Echocardiogram
exhibits.content: Large mobile vegetation (1.2 cm) on the Tricuspid Valve.

steps.prompt: Match the dermatological sign to its description.
rows: Painful, raised red nodes on finger pads
Painless flat red spots on palms/soles
steps.rationale: Oslers = Ouch (Painful/Immune complex). Janeway = Painless (Embolic). Splinter = Embolic. Roth = Embolic/Immunologic.

steps.prompt: Which orders are priority for this patient? Select all that apply.
options: Monitor for signs of Pulmonary Embolism
Place PICC Line for long-term antibiotics
steps.rationale: MRSA requires Vancomycin. Tricuspid vegetations embolize to the LUNGS (PE), not the brain (Stroke), because it's on the right side. Surgery consult is needed for large vegetations. Treatment is 4-6 weeks, so a PICC is needed (though risky in IVDA, it's necessary).

steps.prompt: Patient develops sudden shortness of breath and pleuritic chest pain. SpO2 drops to 88%. Prioritize.
steps.rationale: Right-sided endocarditis (Tricuspid, common in IVDA) shoots emboli to the lungs. This causes septic pulmonary emboli. Oxygen is priority. Anticoagulation (Heparin) is controversial/contraindicated in infective endocarditis due to cerebral bleed risk (mycotic aneurysm).
